Gaussian processes (GP) can be understood as the 'Gaussian distribution over functions'

A GP is defined as a stochastic process where the joint distribution of any finite random variables is Gaussian.
A GP f ∼ GP(µ, k) satisfies fn = [f (x1),··· , f (xn)]T ∼ N (µn, Kn,n) with mean µn = [µ(x1),··· , µ(xn)]T.
A GP f ∼ GP(µ, k) satisfies fn = [f (x1),··· , f (xn)]T ∼ N (µn, Kn,n) with covariance matrix Kn,n = k(Xn, Xn) ≜ {k(xi, xj)}ij.
For the GP prior, µ(·) is generally assumed to be a constant zero function.

The GP posterior utilizes the relations between the testing data X∗ and the training data Xn to estimate the distribution of the function f at X∗, where the relations are explicitly measured by the kernel function k(·,·).
